subtopic_detail: Recurrent wheeze in toddler: ICS preventer threshold
stem: A 20-month-old boy presents for review after his third episode of wheeze in 6 months. Each episode was triggered by a viral upper respiratory infection and responded to salbutamol. Between episodes he is well with no nocturnal cough or exercise limitation. His mother has a diagnosis of asthma. He has no personal history of eczema or food allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 28, HR 120, SpO2 98% on room air, T 36.8 C
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, no wheeze currently, no eczema, growth on 50th centile for weight and length
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol MDI via spacer PRN during acute episodes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence low-dose ICS via spacer for an 8-week trial period
options.rationale: Three or more episodes of viral-induced wheeze in 12 months with a positive modified Asthma Predictive Index (maternal asthma) meets the threshold for a low-dose ICS trial per Australian guidelines. An 8-week duration allows adequate response assessment. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Continue salbutamol PRN alone and reassess in another 3 months
options.rationale: Three episodes in 6 months with a positive API exceeds the threshold for ongoing PRN management alone. Delaying a preventer trial risks further exacerbations and hospital presentations. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence montelukast 4 mg nightly as first-line preventer
options.rationale: Montelukast is second-line to low-dose ICS in young children with recurrent wheeze. Australian guidelines recommend ICS trial first due to superior efficacy evidence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Refer for skin prick allergy testing before starting therapy
options.rationale: Atopic status informs prognosis but does not need confirmation before initiating a preventer trial. The clinical history and positive API are sufficient to begin treatment. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

options.text: Commence high-dose ICS via spacer for a rapid 4-week trial
options.rationale: High-dose ICS is reserved for acute flares or step-up after failed low-dose trial. Starting at high dose in mild recurrent wheeze carries unnecessary adrenal suppression risk without additional benefit. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia)

explanation.summary: Recurrent viral-induced wheeze in preschool children is common and poses a management challenge. The modified Asthma Predictive Index helps identify children likely to benefit from preventer therapy. Australian guidelines recommend a low-dose ICS trial when there are 3 or more wheezing episodes in 12 months with risk factors. The trial should last 8 weeks before assessing response, and ICS should be ceased if no clear benefit is demonstrated.
key_points: Modified API: 1 major (parental asthma, personal eczema) or 2 minor (allergic rhinitis, wheeze without colds, eosinophilia >4%)
Low-dose ICS via spacer with mask is first-line preventer for preschool recurrent wheeze
Trial duration of 8 weeks allows adequate assessment before deciding to continue or cease
Montelukast is second-line, reserved for children unable to use or failing ICS
explanation.pearls: A positive modified API in a preschool child with recurrent wheeze predicts a 77% chance of active asthma at school age, making an ICS trial appropriate even without formal spirometry or allergy testing.

subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa: refeeding syndrome prevention
stem: A 15-year-old girl is admitted to a paediatric ward with a 4-month history of progressive weight loss. Her parents report she has been eating less than 500 kcal per day for the past 2 weeks. Her pre-morbid weight was 58 kg and current weight is 42 kg. She appears emaciated and lethargic.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 48, BP 85/55 supine (postural drop 20 mmHg on standing), T 35.4 C, RR 14
data_table.Investigations: Na 138, K 3.1, PO4 0.62, Mg 0.68, Ca corrected 2.18, glucose 3.6 mmol/L, albumin 30 g/L, ECG: sinus bradycardia with prolonged QTc 470 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial nutritional approach?

options.text: Start nasogastric continuous feeds at full estimated requirements
options.rationale: Full caloric loading in a severely malnourished patient risks life-threatening refeeding syndrome with precipitous falls in phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. Gradual reintroduction is essential. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Eating Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Eating Disorders

options.text: Begin oral intake at 1200 kcal/day with prophylactic phosphate supplement
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend starting at approximately 1200 kcal/day (or 20 kcal/kg/day of current weight) in high-risk patients, with prophylactic phosphate and thiamine supplementation. This balances nutritional rehabilitation with refeeding risk mitigation. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Start oral nutritional supplements at 2000 kcal/day with monitoring
options.rationale: Starting at 2000 kcal/day is too aggressive in a severely malnourished adolescent with electrolyte derangement. This exceeds safe initial caloric targets and significantly increases refeeding risk. (Source: NICE: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence total parenteral nutrition via a central venous catheter
options.rationale: TPN is rarely indicated in eating disorders and carries catheter-related risks. Enteral nutrition via oral or nasogastric route is preferred. TPN may paradoxically worsen refeeding risk due to rapid electrolyte shifts. (Source: RCH CPG: Eating Disorders)

options.text: Commence enteral feeds at 500 kcal/day and increase over two weeks
options.rationale: While cautious, 500 kcal/day is excessively conservative and risks ongoing malnutrition. Australian protocols recommend 1200 kcal/day as the starting point for high-risk patients, with stepwise increases to goal over 5-7 days. (Source: RCH CPG: Eating Disorders)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome is the most dangerous complication of nutritional rehabilitation in severe anorexia nervosa. It occurs due to a shift from catabolic to anabolic metabolism, driving intracellular movement of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. Australian hospital protocols recommend starting at approximately 1200 kcal/day with prophylactic phosphate, thiamine, and electrolyte monitoring every 6-12 hours for the first 72 hours. Caloric intake is increased by 200-300 kcal every 2 days as tolerated.
key_points: High refeeding risk: BMI <14, intake <500 kcal/day for >5 days, baseline electrolyte derangement
Start 1200 kcal/day (approximately 20 kcal/kg current weight) with stepwise increases
Prophylactic phosphate (0.5-1 mmol/kg/day), thiamine 200-300 mg daily, and multivitamins
Monitor electrolytes 6-12 hourly for first 72 hours, then daily
explanation.pearls: Refeeding hypophosphataemia typically nadirs at 48-72 hours after nutritional reintroduction; a phosphate drop of >0.16 mmol/L from baseline within 72 hours mandates caloric restriction back to the starting level until phosphate stabilises.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Eating Disorders

subtopic_detail: Obstructive sleep apnoea: surgical criteria
stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his GP for evaluation of chronic snoring. His parents report nightly snoring with observed apnoeic pauses for the past 12 months. He has daytime mouth-breathing, hyperactivity at school, and bedwetting that had previously resolved. He has had 4 episodes of tonsillitis in the past year. His growth has plateaued, crossing from the 50th to the 25th centile for weight.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 90, RR 20, SpO2 97% awake, weight 17 kg (25th centile)
data_table.Examination: Grade 3+ tonsils bilaterally, adenoid facies, high-arched palate, no craniofacial abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Polysomnography: obstructive AHI 8.2 events/hour, SpO2 nadir 84%, ETCO2 peak 55 mmHg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Trial of intranasal mometasone and montelukast for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Medical therapy with intranasal corticosteroids and montelukast is appropriate for mild OSA (AHI 1-5). With moderate-severe OSA (AHI 8.2), significant desaturations, and growth faltering, surgery is first-line. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Commence nocturnal CPAP therapy and reassess in 6 months
options.rationale: CPAP is reserved for children who are not surgical candidates or who have residual OSA after adenotonsillectomy. In an otherwise healthy child with adenotonsillar hypertrophy, surgery is first-line. CPAP adherence in 5-year-olds is poor. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Refer for adenotonsillectomy with post-operative monitoring plan
options.rationale: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line treatment for moderate-severe paediatric OSA with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. AHI >5, SpO2 nadir <90%, growth faltering, and behavioural symptoms all support surgical intervention. Post-operative monitoring is required given the severity. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

options.text: Repeat polysomnography in 6 months before deciding on surgery
options.rationale: Delaying treatment in a child with moderate-severe OSA, desaturations to 84%, and growth faltering is inappropriate. The PSG already provides clear surgical criteria. Repeat testing would delay necessary intervention. (Source: Australasian Sleep Association)

options.text: Refer for tonsillectomy alone preserving the adenoid tissue
options.rationale: In paediatric OSA with grade 3+ tonsils and adenoid facies, both adenoid and tonsillar tissue contribute to obstruction. Adenotonsillectomy is more effective than tonsillectomy alone for resolution of OSA. (Source: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand)
source_refs: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

explanation.summary: Paediatric obstructive sleep apnoea affects 1-5% of children, with peak prevalence at 2-8 years. Adenotonsillar hypertrophy is the most common cause. Australian guidelines recommend adenotonsillectomy as first-line for moderate-severe OSA (AHI >5) in otherwise healthy children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. Medical therapy is reserved for mild OSA or as an adjunct. Post-operative PSG is recommended for children with severe OSA to confirm resolution.
key_points: Moderate-severe paediatric OSA: AHI >5 events/hour on polysomnography
Adenotonsillectomy cures OSA in approximately 75% of otherwise healthy children
Indications for post-operative monitoring: AHI >10, SpO2 nadir <80%, age <3, obesity, craniofacial anomaly
Behavioural symptoms (hyperactivity, enuresis) often resolve after successful surgery
explanation.pearls: Secondary enuresis in a child with snoring should prompt evaluation for OSA; the mechanism involves increased atrial natriuretic peptide secretion from negative intrathoracic pressure swings, causing nocturnal polyuria.

sources.label: Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand

subtopic_detail: Infant GORD: red flags prompting investigation
stem: A 6-week-old exclusively breastfed girl is brought to your clinic with frequent posseting after feeds. Her mother reports she arches her back during feeds, has episodes of irritability after lying flat, and has had two episodes of green-tinged vomiting in the past week. She was born at term via normal vaginal delivery with no neonatal complications.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 145, RR 38, T 36.9 C, weight 3.9 kg (birth weight 3.5 kg, now tracking below 10th centile)
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no palpable mass, mildly distended, normal bowel sounds, no bile staining on nappy
data_table.Investigations: Not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Trial of 4-week omeprazole and review clinical response
options.rationale: Empirical acid suppression without investigation is inappropriate when bilious vomiting is present. Bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical emergency until proven otherwise and requires urgent imaging to exclude malrotation. (Source: RCH CPG: Vomiting in Infants)

options.text: 24-hour oesophageal pH monitoring with impedance study
options.rationale: pH-impedance testing quantifies reflux burden but does not address the critical concern of bilious vomiting. Malrotation must be excluded first as it is a time-critical surgical diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Vomiting in Infants)

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound to assess the pylorus and gut anatomy
options.rationale: Ultrasound is the investigation of choice for pyloric stenosis (non-bilious projectile vomiting) but is not the appropriate first-line test for suspected malrotation. The position of the SMA and SMV can be assessed but an upper GI contrast study is the gold standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Malrotation)

options.text: Urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study within 24 hours
options.rationale: Bilious vomiting in a neonate or young infant mandates urgent exclusion of malrotation with volvulus. An upper GI contrast study is the gold standard, demonstrating the position of the duodenojejunal flexure. This should not be delayed for other investigations. (Source: RCH CPG: Malrotation)

options.text: Reassurance with feeding advice and weight check in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance is inappropriate when red flags are present: bilious vomiting, weight faltering, and back-arching. These features mandate investigation rather than watchful waiting. Bilious vomiting alone warrants urgent surgical evaluation. (Source: NICE: GORD in Children)

explanation.summary: While gastro-oesophageal reflux is common and usually benign in infants, bilious (green) vomiting is a critical red flag that mandates urgent investigation for malrotation with volvulus. Malrotation occurs in approximately 1 in 500 live births and can present with midgut volvulus at any age, most commonly in the first month of life. An upper GI contrast study is the gold standard investigation, and surgical correction (Ladd procedure) should not be delayed if volvulus is confirmed.
key_points: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise
Upper GI contrast study is the gold standard for diagnosing malrotation
Red flags in infant vomiting: bilious, projectile, haematemesis, weight faltering, abdominal distension
Malrotation with volvulus requires emergency Ladd procedure to prevent bowel necrosis
explanation.pearls: The duodenojejunal flexure (ligament of Treitz) should sit to the left of the left pedicle of L2 on an upper GI contrast study; failure to reach this position has a sensitivity of 95% for malrotation even in the absence of active volvulus.

subtopic_detail: Chronic functional constipation: disimpaction protocol
stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of infrequent hard stools, passage every 4-5 days, and intermittent faecal soiling. His parents report that he withholds stool, crossing his legs and hiding behind furniture. He was previously toilet-trained at age 2.5 years. Dietary history reveals low fibre and fluid intake. There are no red flags for organic disease.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with a palpable faecal mass in the left lower quadrant, normal anal position and tone, no perianal fissures
data_table.Investigations: Not required for clinical diagnosis

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.text: Dietary modification with increased fibre and fluids alone
options.rationale: Dietary modification alone is insufficient when faecal impaction is present. Disimpaction must be achieved before maintenance therapy can be effective. Diet changes are important but adjunctive. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Daily lactulose 10 mL twice daily for ongoing maintenance
options.rationale: Lactulose is an osmotic laxative suitable for maintenance but has insufficient potency for disimpaction of an established faecal mass. Disimpaction requires high-dose macrogol before stepping down to maintenance. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Phosphate enema followed by daily senna for 3 months
options.rationale: Rectal interventions should be avoided in children with withholding behaviour as they can worsen anxiety and stool refusal. Oral disimpaction with macrogol is preferred. Stimulant laxatives alone are second-line for maintenance. (Source: NICE: Constipation in Children)

options.text: Abdominal X-ray to confirm impaction then plan treatment
options.rationale: Abdominal X-ray is not required when clinical assessment confirms faecal impaction (palpable mass). Routine imaging adds radiation exposure without changing management and delays the start of treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Oral macrogol disimpaction regimen then daily macrogol maintenance
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend oral macrogol (PEG 3350) escalating-dose disimpaction over 3-7 days, followed by long-term daily macrogol maintenance for at least 6 months. Oral disimpaction avoids the psychological harm of rectal interventions in a child with withholding behaviour. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Chronic functional constipation with faecal impaction and overflow soiling is common in preschool children, particularly those with stool withholding behaviour. Management follows a two-phase approach: disimpaction first, then long-term maintenance. Australian guidelines recommend oral macrogol (polyethylene glycol 3350) for both phases. The disimpaction phase uses escalating doses over 3-7 days until soft stools are passed. Maintenance continues for at least 6 months with gradual weaning, supported by toileting behaviour programs and dietary modification.
key_points: Disimpaction before maintenance: macrogol escalating dose over 3-7 days
Avoid rectal interventions in children with withholding behaviour to prevent worsening anxiety
Maintenance macrogol for minimum 6 months with slow dose reduction
Soiling in a previously toilet-trained child with constipation is overflow incontinence, not behavioural
explanation.pearls: Macrogol (PEG 3350) disimpaction dosing in children: start at 1 sachet/day and escalate by 1 sachet per day to a maximum of 4 sachets/day for 1-5 year-olds (8 sachets/day for >5 years), continuing the peak dose until soft stools are achieved.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric Crohn disease: escalation after 5-ASA failure
stem: A 14-year-old boy with ileocolonic Crohn disease diagnosed 8 months ago returns with worsening abdominal pain, bloody diarrhoea, and 3 kg weight loss over 6 weeks despite adherent mesalazine therapy. His linear growth has decelerated, crossing from the 50th to the 25th centile for height over the past year. He has no perianal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 110/65, T 37.2 C, weight 45 kg (25th centile)
data_table.Investigations: CRP 42 mg/L, ESR 38, albumin 28 g/L, faecal calprotectin 1850 mcg/g, Hb 108 g/L, MCV 74 fL

lead_in: What is the most appropriate change to his management?

options.text: Commence exclusive enteral nutrition for 8 weeks as induction
options.rationale: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) for 6-8 weeks is the preferred first-line induction therapy for active luminal Crohn disease in paediatric patients per ECCO-ESPGHAN guidelines and Australian practice. EEN achieves mucosal healing rates comparable to corticosteroids while supporting growth. (Source: ECCO-ESPGHAN Paediatric Crohn Guidelines)

options.text: Add prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day tapering course for 8 weeks
options.rationale: While corticosteroids induce remission, they impair linear growth and bone mineralisation - critical concerns in an adolescent with growth deceleration. EEN is preferred in paediatric Crohn disease for its equivalent efficacy and pro-growth effect. (Source: ECCO-ESPGHAN Paediatric Crohn Guidelines)

options.text: Start azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day as maintenance immunomodulator
options.rationale: Azathioprine is an appropriate maintenance agent after remission induction but takes 8-12 weeks to reach therapeutic effect. It cannot achieve rapid remission induction and should be started concurrently with induction therapy rather than alone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal)

options.text: Escalate mesalazine to maximum dose of 4 g daily with review
options.rationale: Mesalazine has limited efficacy in ileal and severe Crohn disease. Dose escalation is unlikely to achieve remission given the significant inflammatory burden (calprotectin >1800, elevated CRP, hypoalbuminaemia). Escalation of therapy class is needed. (Source: ECCO-ESPGHAN Paediatric Crohn Guidelines)

options.text: Start infliximab induction at 5 mg/kg intravenously now
options.rationale: Anti-TNF therapy is effective but is typically reserved as second-line induction after EEN failure or for high-risk features (deep ulceration, extensive disease, perianal fistulae). In the absence of these, EEN should be trialled first. (Source: ECCO-ESPGHAN Paediatric Crohn Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Exclusive enteral nutrition is a unique first-line induction therapy in paediatric Crohn disease, distinguishing paediatric from adult management. EEN achieves mucosal healing in 60-80% of children while providing optimal nutrition for linear growth. It is delivered as a polymeric formula for 6-8 weeks with complete dietary exclusion. Following EEN induction, maintenance therapy with an immunomodulator (thiopurine or methotrexate) is typically introduced to sustain remission.
key_points: EEN is first-line induction for active paediatric luminal Crohn disease per international guidelines
EEN achieves equivalent remission rates to corticosteroids without growth-suppressive effects
Duration is 6-8 weeks of exclusive polymeric formula with gradual food reintroduction
Growth deceleration is a key indication to avoid corticosteroids in paediatric Crohn disease
explanation.pearls: Faecal calprotectin >800 mcg/g in a child with known Crohn disease has a >90% positive predictive value for endoscopically active disease and supports the decision to escalate therapy without repeating endoscopy.

subtopic_detail: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia: initial stabilisation
stem: A male infant is born at 38 weeks gestation via emergency caesarean section for foetal distress. A left-sided congenital diaphragmatic hernia was diagnosed on antenatal ultrasound at 20 weeks. At birth he is cyanotic and in respiratory distress. The left hemithorax has absent breath sounds and the abdomen is scaphoid. He is intubated in the delivery suite.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 160, SpO2 78% pre-ductal on FiO2 1.0, BP 45/28 (MAP 34)
data_table.Investigations: Pre-ductal ABG: pH 7.18, pCO2 62, pO2 42, BE -8, lactate 5.2 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Intubated, left hemithorax dull to percussion with absent breath sounds, heart sounds displaced to the right, scaphoid abdomen

lead_in: What is the single most important initial intervention?

options.text: Immediate surgical repair of the diaphragmatic defect now
options.rationale: Immediate surgery is no longer recommended. Current evidence supports a period of pre-operative stabilisation to optimise gas exchange and haemodynamics before repair. Operating on an unstable neonate increases mortality. (Source: CDH EURO Consortium Guidelines)

options.text: Insert a large-bore orogastric tube on continuous drainage
options.rationale: A large-bore orogastric or nasogastric tube on continuous low-pressure suction is the critical first step to decompress the herniated bowel in the chest, preventing progressive mediastinal shift and allowing lung expansion. This should occur immediately after intubation. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: CDH)

options.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm for pulmonary vasodilation
options.rationale: Inhaled nitric oxide may be considered for refractory pulmonary hypertension but is not the immediate first intervention. Gastric decompression to reduce intrathoracic bowel distension is more urgent and may itself improve oxygenation by reducing mediastinal shift. (Source: CDH EURO Consortium Guidelines)

options.text: Insert a left intercostal chest drain to relieve pneumothorax
options.rationale: A chest drain is contraindicated in CDH as it risks injuring herniated abdominal viscera within the thorax. The absent breath sounds and dullness are from bowel, not air. Chest drain insertion before imaging confirmation would be dangerous. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: CDH)

options.text: Commence bag-mask ventilation with high-pressure support
options.rationale: Bag-mask ventilation is contraindicated in CDH as it insufflates the herniated stomach and bowel, worsening mediastinal compression. These infants should be intubated immediately and never receive bag-mask ventilation. (Source: ANZCOR Neonatal Resuscitation Guideline)

explanation.summary: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia management has shifted from emergency surgery to a stabilise-first approach. Initial priorities are endotracheal intubation (avoiding bag-mask ventilation), gastric decompression with a large-bore orogastric tube, and gentle ventilation with permissive hypercapnoea (target pCO2 <65 mmHg). Pre-operative stabilisation over 24-48 hours allows assessment of pulmonary hypertension severity and optimisation before definitive repair.
key_points: Never use bag-mask ventilation in CDH: immediate intubation is required
Large-bore orogastric tube on continuous drainage is the critical first intervention
Gentle ventilation: peak inspiratory pressure <25 cmH2O, permissive hypercapnoea
Surgery is delayed until haemodynamic stability is achieved, typically 24-48 hours
explanation.pearls: Pre-ductal SpO2 target in CDH stabilisation is >85% (not >95%); aggressive ventilation to achieve normal saturations causes ventilator-induced lung injury to the hypoplastic lung and worsens pulmonary hypertension.

subtopic_detail: Neonatal abstinence syndrome: pharmacotherapy threshold
stem: A 2-day-old male infant born at 39 weeks to a mother on a methadone maintenance program (80 mg daily) is in the special care nursery for observation. He is breastfeeding but has developed high-pitched crying, tremors when disturbed, frequent sneezing, loose stools, and disrupted sleep cycles. Serial modified Finnegan scores over the past 12 hours have been 9, 11, and 12.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 172, RR 52, T 37.6 C, SpO2 98% RA
data_table.Examination: Jittery with increased tone, hyperactive Moro reflex, nasal stuffiness, mild excoriation from facial rubbing, no seizures
data_table.Feeding: Breastfeeding with supplementary expressed breast milk, intake adequate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase non-pharmacological care and rescore in 4 hours
options.rationale: Non-pharmacological measures (swaddling, reduced stimulation, demand feeding) should already be maximised. Three consecutive Finnegan scores of 9, 11, and 12 (two above the pharmacotherapy threshold of 8) indicate these measures are insufficient. Pharmacotherapy is indicated. (Source: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Commence phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading dose intravenously
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is reserved for poly-substance withdrawal (especially benzodiazepines or alcohol) or as second-line adjunctive therapy for opioid NAS refractory to morphine. First-line treatment for opioid NAS is oral morphine. (Source: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Commence oral morphine using a symptom-based dosing protocol
options.rationale: Two or more consecutive modified Finnegan scores of 8 or above despite optimised non-pharmacological care meet the threshold for pharmacotherapy. Oral morphine is first-line for opioid NAS per Australian guidelines, using a symptom-based protocol with dose titration guided by ongoing scores. (Source: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Cease breastfeeding and commence a high-calorie formula
options.rationale: Breastfeeding is encouraged in mothers on stable methadone maintenance who are not using illicit substances. Breast milk contains small amounts of methadone which may help ameliorate withdrawal. Cessation would remove this benefit and the bonding opportunity. (Source: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Transfer to NICU for continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring
options.rationale: NICU transfer is not indicated for NAS unless there are complications such as seizures, severe dehydration, or respiratory compromise. This infant can be managed in the special care nursery with pharmacotherapy and monitoring. (Source: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

explanation.summary: Neonatal abstinence syndrome affects 55-94% of infants exposed to maternal opioids in utero. Australian guidelines use the modified Finnegan Neonatal Abstinence Scoring System to guide pharmacotherapy initiation. Non-pharmacological care is first-line, with pharmacotherapy (oral morphine) commenced when two or more consecutive scores reach 8 or above. Breastfeeding is supported in stable methadone-maintained mothers. The Eat Sleep Console (ESC) model is an alternative assessment tool increasingly used in Australian centres.
key_points: Pharmacotherapy threshold: 2 or more consecutive modified Finnegan scores of 8 or above
Oral morphine is first-line pharmacotherapy for opioid-related NAS in Australia
Breastfeeding is encouraged in stable methadone/buprenorphine-maintained mothers
NAS onset is typically 24-72 hours for heroin, and up to 7 days for methadone
explanation.pearls: Methadone-exposed neonates have a later onset of NAS (48-72 hours, peak at day 4-5) compared to heroin (24-48 hours) due to methadone's longer half-life; early discharge before day 5 risks missing the withdrawal peak.

sources.label: NSW Health: NAS Clinical Practice Guideline

subtopic_detail: Meconium ileus: investigation pathway for cystic fibrosis
stem: A 36-hour-old term neonate has not passed meconium and has developed progressive abdominal distension with bilious vomiting. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. The newborn bloodspot screening (immunoreactive trypsinogen) result is pending. Antenatal ultrasound at 32 weeks showed echogenic bowel.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 155, RR 48, T 36.7 C, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Grossly distended abdomen, visible bowel loops, no hernia, rectum empty on gentle examination
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal X-ray: multiple dilated loops of small bowel, no air-fluid levels, ground-glass opacity in right lower quadrant, no distal gas in rectum

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a rectal tube and administer warm saline washouts
options.rationale: Rectal washouts may help in meconium plug syndrome or Hirschsprung disease but are ineffective for meconium ileus where the obstruction is in the distal ileum. The inspissated meconium requires a different approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Surgical Conditions)

options.text: Arrange urgent laparotomy for small bowel obstruction now
options.rationale: The radiographic pattern of ground-glass opacity without air-fluid levels is characteristic of simple meconium ileus, not atresia. Atresia typically shows distinct air-fluid levels with a transition point. A contrast enema should be attempted before surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Surgical Conditions)

options.text: Observe with IV fluids and await newborn screening results
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is inappropriate in a neonate with progressive bilious vomiting and complete distal obstruction. The diagnosis needs confirmation and therapeutic intervention regardless of the NBS result, which takes several days. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Bowel Obstruction)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Bowel Obstruction

options.text: Therapeutic water-soluble contrast enema under fluoroscopy
options.rationale: A Gastrografin (water-soluble contrast) enema is both diagnostic and potentially therapeutic in simple meconium ileus. The hyperosmolar contrast draws fluid into the bowel lumen, softening the inspissated meconium. It confirms the microcolon and may relieve the obstruction in up to 50% of cases, avoiding surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Surgical Conditions)

options.text: Commence oral N-acetylcysteine to dissolve the meconium plug
options.rationale: Oral N-acetylcysteine cannot be administered to a vomiting neonate with bowel obstruction. While NAC can thin inspissated meconium, it is used post-operatively or via the enema route, not orally in the setting of obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Surgical Conditions)

explanation.summary: Meconium ileus is the presenting feature of cystic fibrosis in 10-20% of affected neonates. The inspissated meconium obstructs the distal ileum, presenting with failure to pass meconium, abdominal distension, and bilious vomiting. Abdominal X-ray characteristically shows dilated bowel without air-fluid levels and a ground-glass appearance. A Gastrografin enema is both diagnostic (confirming microcolon) and therapeutic (relieving obstruction in simple cases). All neonates with meconium ileus require sweat chloride testing and CFTR gene analysis regardless of NBS results.
key_points: Meconium ileus: suspect CF in any neonate with failure to pass meconium and echogenic bowel history
Gastrografin enema is first-line: diagnostic and therapeutic in simple meconium ileus
Surgical intervention required for complicated meconium ileus (volvulus, atresia, perforation)
Sweat chloride test after 2 weeks of age and CFTR genotyping for definitive CF diagnosis
explanation.pearls: The absence of air-fluid levels on abdominal X-ray in a neonate with distal bowel obstruction is a hallmark of meconium ileus; the highly viscous meconium prevents layering of air and fluid, producing the characteristic ground-glass appearance instead.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Neonatal Bowel Obstruction

subtopic_detail: Central precocious puberty: GnRH agonist indication
stem: A 6-year-old girl is referred by her GP for breast development noticed by her mother 4 months ago. She has no history of adrenal disease or exposure to exogenous oestrogens. She is the tallest girl in her class but her mid-parental height prediction is 50th centile. There is no headache or visual disturbance.

data_table.Examination: Tanner breast stage 3, Tanner pubic hair stage 2, no axillary hair, no acne, height 128 cm (>97th centile for age), weight 27 kg (90th centile)
data_table.Investigations: Bone age X-ray: 9 years (chronological age 6.3 years), basal LH 1.8 IU/L, basal FSH 4.2 IU/L, oestradiol 120 pmol/L, pelvic ultrasound: uterine length 42 mm with endometrial echo, bilateral ovarian volumes 3.5 mL, MRI brain: normal pituitary and hypothalamus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure and monitor growth with 6-monthly bone age imaging
options.rationale: Observation alone risks significant final height loss. With bone age advanced by 2.7 years and progressive Tanner staging, the growth plates will fuse prematurely. Active intervention is warranted to preserve adult height potential. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Commence cyproterone acetate to suppress androgen effects
options.rationale: Cyproterone acetate is an anti-androgen used in peripheral precocious puberty (e.g. congenital adrenal hyperplasia). In central precocious puberty, GnRH agonist therapy is the appropriate treatment as it suppresses the central HPG axis. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Perform GnRH stimulation test before initiating any treatment
options.rationale: A GnRH stimulation test is indicated when the diagnosis is uncertain (e.g. borderline LH, Tanner stage 2 only). This child has clear clinical and biochemical evidence of central precocious puberty: Tanner 3 breasts, advanced bone age, elevated oestradiol, uterine maturation, and normal MRI. Treatment can proceed. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Commence letrozole to suppress peripheral oestrogen synthesis
options.rationale: Aromatase inhibitors like letrozole are used in peripheral precocious puberty (McCune-Albright syndrome) or as adjunctive therapy. In central precocious puberty with a normal MRI, GnRH agonist is first-line as it suppresses the inappropriately activated HPG axis. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline)

options.text: Commence GnRH agonist therapy with 3-monthly depot injection
options.rationale: GnRH agonist (e.g. leuprorelin depot) is the standard treatment for central precocious puberty in girls under 8 years. This child meets all criteria: progressive pubertal development before age 8, advanced bone age >2 years, and confirmed central activation with normal MRI. Treatment suppresses the HPG axis, halting puberty and preserving final adult height. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

explanation.summary: Central precocious puberty is defined as onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in girls or age 9 in boys, driven by premature activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Investigation includes bone age, basal gonadotropins, sex steroids, pelvic ultrasound, and MRI brain. GnRH agonist therapy is indicated when there is progressive puberty with significantly advanced bone age, to halt pubertal progression and optimise adult height. Treatment is typically continued until the average age of puberty onset (approximately age 11 in girls).
key_points: Central precocious puberty: puberty before age 8 in girls, age 9 in boys, with central HPG axis activation
MRI brain is mandatory to exclude intracranial pathology (hypothalamic hamartoma, glioma)
GnRH agonist therapy preserves adult height by slowing epiphyseal fusion
Treatment is discontinued at approximately age 11 in girls, allowing normal pubertal progression
explanation.pearls: In girls with central precocious puberty, a bone age advancement of >2 years beyond chronological age predicts significant adult height compromise of 5-12 cm if untreated; GnRH agonist therapy recovers approximately 4-7 cm of predicted adult height when started before age 7.

subtopic_detail: Congenital hypothyroidism: levothyroxine dose adjustment
stem: A 3-week-old girl was diagnosed with congenital hypothyroidism on newborn bloodspot screening (elevated TSH) and commenced on levothyroxine 12 mcg/kg/day at day 10 of life. She was born at term with a birth weight of 3.4 kg. Her mother asks about the follow-up plan at a 2-week review appointment. She is feeding well and growing normally.

data_table.Investigations: Day 10 confirmatory: TSH 85 mIU/L (ref <10), free T4 6.2 pmol/L (ref 12-22). Today (day 24): TSH 18 mIU/L, free T4 22 pmol/L
data_table.Examination: Alert, no prolonged jaundice, anterior fontanelle normal, no goitre, normal tone

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her thyroid replacement?

options.text: Increase levothyroxine dose and recheck TFTs in 2 weeks
options.rationale: The TSH remains elevated at 18 mIU/L despite 2 weeks of therapy, indicating insufficient replacement. The target is normalisation of TSH to <5 mIU/L and free T4 in the upper half of the reference range within 2-4 weeks. The dose should be increased and rechecked in 2 weeks. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group: Congenital Hypothyroidism Guideline)

options.text: Continue current dose as free T4 is now within normal range
options.rationale: While free T4 has normalised, the TSH remains elevated, indicating ongoing hypothalamic-pituitary feedback that the peripheral thyroid hormone level is insufficient. Both TSH and free T4 must be optimised to prevent neurodevelopmental harm. (Source: APEG: Congenital Hypothyroidism Guideline)

options.text: Reduce levothyroxine as free T4 is at the upper reference limit
options.rationale: Reducing the dose would be counterproductive with an elevated TSH. In congenital hypothyroidism, the target free T4 is the upper half of the reference range. A free T4 of 22 pmol/L is appropriate; the TSH needs to fall further. (Source: APEG: Congenital Hypothyroidism Guideline)

options.text: Order a thyroid scintigraphy scan before adjusting dosing
options.rationale: Thyroid scintigraphy can define the aetiology (athyreosis, ectopia, dyshormonogenesis) but does not change acute management. The priority is optimising thyroid replacement to protect neurodevelopment. Imaging can be arranged electively. (Source: APEG: Congenital Hypothyroidism Guideline)

options.text: Cease levothyroxine and recheck TFTs in 4 weeks to confirm
options.rationale: Ceasing thyroid replacement in a confirmed case of congenital hypothyroidism risks severe neurodevelopmental harm. Trial off treatment is only considered at age 3 years in selected cases where the diagnosis is uncertain (e.g. ectopic gland with borderline results). (Source: APEG: Congenital Hypothyroidism Guideline)

explanation.summary: Congenital hypothyroidism is the most common preventable cause of intellectual disability, detected by newborn screening in approximately 1 in 3000-4000 births in Australia. Levothyroxine is commenced at 10-15 mcg/kg/day and adjusted to normalise TSH (<5 mIU/L) and maintain free T4 in the upper half of the reference range. Rapid normalisation within 2-4 weeks is critical for neurodevelopmental outcomes. Thyroid function tests are monitored at 2 weeks post-initiation, then every 1-2 months in the first year.
key_points: Treatment target: TSH <5 mIU/L and free T4 in upper half of reference range within 2-4 weeks
Starting dose: levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day, given as crushed tablet with small amount of water
Monitoring: TFTs at 2 weeks post-start, then every 1-2 months in first year, every 2-3 months until age 3
Trial off therapy only at age 3 years in selected uncertain cases
explanation.pearls: In congenital hypothyroidism, the free T4 often normalises before the TSH; dose adjustments should be guided by TSH until it is <5 mIU/L, even if the free T4 appears adequate or slightly elevated.

subtopic_detail: Short stature: growth hormone stimulation test indication
stem: An 8-year-old boy is referred for short stature. His height is 116 cm (below 3rd centile) and his growth velocity over the past year has been 3.5 cm/year (normal >5 cm/year at this age). His mid-parental height prediction is 50th centile. He was born SGA at 2.4 kg but had adequate catch-up growth by age 2. He has no chronic illness, his diet is adequate, and he is prepubertal.

data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, ESR 4, coeliac serology negative, TFTs normal (TSH 2.1, fT4 16), IGF-1 78 ng/mL (low for age), bone age 6 years (delayed by 2 years)
data_table.Examination: Proportionate short stature, no dysmorphic features, no midline defects, Tanner stage 1

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: MRI of the pituitary gland and hypothalamus with contrast
options.rationale: MRI pituitary is indicated after confirming GH deficiency with stimulation testing. Imaging before biochemical confirmation would lead to unnecessary investigation in children with constitutional delay or other causes of low IGF-1. (Source: APEG: Growth Hormone Guidelines)

options.text: Growth hormone stimulation test with two pharmacological agents
options.rationale: The combination of height below 3rd centile, poor growth velocity (<5 cm/year), low IGF-1, and significantly delayed bone age raises strong suspicion for growth hormone deficiency. Australian guidelines require two separate GH stimulation tests (e.g. glucagon + arginine or clonidine) to confirm the diagnosis before treatment. (Source: APEG: Growth Hormone Guidelines)

options.text: Karyotype analysis to exclude Turner syndrome or variant
options.rationale: Karyotype is essential in short girls to exclude Turner syndrome, but this is a boy with proportionate short stature and no dysmorphic features. Karyotype would not be the priority investigation here; GH axis assessment is more appropriate. (Source: APEG: Growth Hormone Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure that this represents constitutional delay of growth
options.rationale: While the delayed bone age is consistent with constitutional delay, the low IGF-1 and poor growth velocity below 3.5 cm/year cannot be dismissed as constitutional delay alone. GH deficiency must be excluded with stimulation testing before attributing the pattern to constitutional delay. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Commence a 6-month trial of growth hormone without testing
options.rationale: GH therapy requires confirmed GH deficiency via stimulation testing for PBS funding in Australia. An empirical trial without biochemical confirmation is not appropriate and would not be subsidised. Two failed stimulation tests are required. (Source: APEG: Growth Hormone Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Short stature investigation follows a systematic approach: exclude chronic disease, assess growth velocity, check bone age, and measure IGF-1/IGFBP-3. When GH deficiency is suspected (low IGF-1, poor growth velocity, short stature), two pharmacological GH stimulation tests are required for confirmation per Australian PBS criteria. A peak GH response below 10 mIU/L (some centres use 7 mcg/L) on both tests confirms deficiency. MRI pituitary is then performed to identify structural abnormalities.
key_points: GH deficiency diagnosis requires two failed stimulation tests (peak GH <10 mIU/L)
Common stimulation agents: glucagon, arginine, clonidine, insulin (ITT rarely used in children)
Low IGF-1 + poor growth velocity + delayed bone age = strong suspicion for GH deficiency
PBS-funded GH therapy requires confirmed deficiency and height below 3rd centile
explanation.pearls: IGF-1 levels must be interpreted against age-specific and puberty-specific reference ranges; a prepubertal child with a low-normal IGF-1 for age may have GH deficiency masked by the physiologically lower prepubertal range.

subtopic_detail: Graves disease in adolescent: antithyroid drug choice
stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with a 3-month history of weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, tremor, and difficulty concentrating at school. Her mother noticed a neck swelling. She has no eye symptoms. There is no family history of autoimmune disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 (sinus tachycardia), BP 130/60, T 37.2 C
data_table.Examination: Diffuse smooth goitre with a bruit, fine tremor of outstretched hands, lid lag but no proptosis, brisk reflexes
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01 mIU/L (ref 0.5-5.0), free T4 58 pmol/L (ref 12-22), free T3 18.2 pmol/L (ref 3.5-6.5), TSH receptor antibodies strongly positive, FBC and LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Propylthiouracil 100 mg three times daily with LFT monitoring
options.rationale: Propylthiouracil is contraindicated as first-line in children under 16 due to the risk of severe hepatotoxicity (including fatal hepatic failure). Australian and international guidelines mandate carbimazole as first-line in paediatric Graves disease. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Radioactive iodine ablation after 2 weeks of beta-blocker
options.rationale: Radioactive iodine is generally avoided in children under 15 years due to radiation exposure concerns and is reserved for relapsed Graves disease after failed antithyroid drug therapy. Initial medical therapy should be trialled first. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Carbimazole 0.5 mg/kg/day plus propranolol for symptom relief
options.rationale: Carbimazole is first-line antithyroid therapy in paediatric Graves disease at 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 30 mg/day), combined with a beta-blocker (propranolol) for symptom control during the 4-6 weeks before carbimazole reaches full effect. PTU is contraindicated as first-line due to hepatotoxicity risk. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

options.text: Referral for total thyroidectomy as definitive first treatment
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for children who fail or are intolerant of antithyroid drug therapy, have large goitres with compressive symptoms, or have suspected malignancy. A trial of antithyroid medication for 12-24 months is the standard initial approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Atenolol alone as initial therapy with thyroid monitoring weekly
options.rationale: Beta-blockers provide symptomatic relief only and do not address the underlying hyperthyroidism. Without antithyroid drug therapy, the free T4 and T3 will remain dangerously elevated, risking thyroid storm. Beta-blockers are adjunctive, not sole therapy. (Source: Australasian Paediatric Endocrine Group)

explanation.summary: Paediatric Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism in children. Carbimazole (or methimazole) is the first-line antithyroid drug, with propylthiouracil contraindicated as initial therapy due to hepatotoxicity risk. Treatment typically involves 12-24 months of antithyroid drug therapy, with remission rates of 20-30% per treatment course. A beta-blocker is added for symptomatic relief during the initial weeks. Monitoring includes FBC for agranulocytosis and LFTs, with urgent FBC if sore throat or fever develops.
key_points: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day is first-line; PTU contraindicated in children under 16
Beta-blocker (propranolol) for symptomatic control until euthyroid
Agranulocytosis risk: urgent FBC if sore throat or fever on carbimazole
Remission rate 20-30% per 12-24 month treatment course
explanation.pearls: PTU carries a 1 in 2000-4000 risk of fatal hepatic failure in children, leading to an FDA black-box warning; carbimazole-related agranulocytosis (1 in 500) is serious but more predictable and usually reversible with drug cessation.

subtopic_detail: Acute rheumatic fever: Australian Jones criteria modification
stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote Northern Territory community presents with fever, migratory polyarthralgia affecting his knees and ankles, and a new cardiac murmur. He had a sore throat 3 weeks ago that was not treated with antibiotics. He has no rash, no subcutaneous nodules, and no chorea.

data_table.Examination: Pan-systolic murmur grade 3/6 at the apex radiating to the axilla, tender swollen left knee and right ankle, no erythema marginatum, no subcutaneous nodules
data_table.Investigations: CRP 68 mg/L, ESR 55, ASO titre 480 IU/mL (ref <200), anti-DNase B 620 IU/mL (ref <300), throat swab: no group A streptococcus isolated, echocardiogram: moderate mitral regurgitation with anterior leaflet prolapse

lead_in: How many major Jones criteria does this child meet under the Australian modification?

options.text: One major criterion: carditis with mitral regurgitation only
options.rationale: Under the standard Jones criteria, polyarthralgia (without frank arthritis) is a minor criterion. However, the Australian modification for high-risk populations accepts polyarthralgia as a major criterion, giving this child two major criteria. (Source: RHDAustralia: ARF Diagnosis Guideline)

options.text: One major and one minor only, insufficient for diagnosis
options.rationale: This child meets the diagnostic threshold. The Australian modification elevates polyarthralgia and monoarthritis to major criteria in high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, Pacific Islander peoples). Combined with carditis, this gives two major criteria. (Source: RHDAustralia: ARF Diagnosis Guideline)

options.text: Three major criteria: carditis plus arthritis plus fever
options.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion under all versions of the Jones criteria. He has polyarthralgia (not frank polyarthritis), which is major only under the Australian high-risk modification. Carditis plus polyarthralgia gives two major criteria. (Source: RHDAustralia: ARF Diagnosis Guideline)

options.text: Two major criteria: carditis and polyarthralgia under AU rules
options.rationale: Under the Australian modification of the Jones criteria for high-risk populations, polyarthralgia (without joint swelling/effusion) is elevated to a major criterion. This child has echocardiographic carditis (major) and polyarthralgia (major under AU modification), meeting the diagnostic threshold of 2 major criteria with evidence of preceding streptococcal infection. (Source: RHDAustralia: ARF Diagnosis Guideline)

options.text: Two major criteria: carditis and polyarthritis as per standard
options.rationale: The child has polyarthralgia (pain without swelling in multiple joints), not polyarthritis (swelling/effusion). Only the left knee and right ankle are swollen, and the description indicates tenderness in others without swelling. The distinction matters because the standard Jones criteria require frank arthritis for a major criterion. (Source: RHDAustralia: ARF Diagnosis Guideline)

explanation.summary: The Australian modification of the Jones criteria was developed by RHDAustralia to improve sensitivity for acute rheumatic fever diagnosis in high-risk populations, where delayed or missed diagnosis leads to worse cardiac outcomes. Key modifications include accepting monoarthritis and polyarthralgia as major criteria, and subclinical carditis (echocardiographic valvulitis without a clinical murmur) as a major criterion. These changes recognise that ARF presentations in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities are often atypical compared to standard teaching descriptions.
key_points: Australian modification elevates polyarthralgia and monoarthritis to major criteria in high-risk populations
High-risk: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples, Pacific Islander peoples, and those in high-incidence settings
Subclinical carditis on echocardiography counts as a major criterion in the Australian modification
ASO titre >480 or anti-DNase B >340 confirms preceding streptococcal infection
explanation.pearls: In Australian high-risk populations, aseptic monoarthritis of a large joint with elevated inflammatory markers and streptococcal serology meets one major criterion - under standard Jones criteria this would only be a minor criterion, potentially causing a missed diagnosis.

sources.label: Heart Foundation of Australia: ARF and RHD Guideline

subtopic_detail: Rheumatic heart disease: secondary prophylaxis duration
stem: A 12-year-old Aboriginal girl from a regional Queensland community had a confirmed episode of acute rheumatic fever at age 10 with mild carditis (mitral regurgitation on echocardiography). She has been on benzathine penicillin G injections every 28 days since diagnosis. Her most recent echocardiogram shows persistent mild mitral regurgitation. Her parents ask how long she needs to continue the injections.

data_table.Investigations: Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation, no mitral stenosis, normal LV function
data_table.Medications: Benzathine penicillin G 900 mg (1.2 million units) IM every 28 days

lead_in: What is the recommended duration of secondary prophylaxis?

options.text: Continue for 5 years from last episode then cease therapy
options.rationale: Five years from the last episode is the minimum duration for ARF without carditis. This child had carditis with persistent valvular disease, which requires a longer duration of prophylaxis. (Source: RHDAustralia: Secondary Prophylaxis Guideline)

options.text: Continue until age 18 then cease if no recurrence of ARF
options.rationale: Age 18 is insufficient for a child with persistent RHD. Australian guidelines recommend prophylaxis until age 21 for ARF with carditis but no persistent RHD, or longer when RHD persists. (Source: RHDAustralia: Secondary Prophylaxis Guideline)

options.text: Cease now as 2 years of prophylaxis is adequate for mild RHD
options.rationale: Two years is insufficient. Recurrence of ARF is highest in the first 5 years and ongoing streptococcal exposure continues to risk further cardiac damage. Minimum duration with any carditis is 10 years or until age 21. (Source: RHDAustralia: Secondary Prophylaxis Guideline)

options.text: Switch to oral penicillin V daily until age 21 for convenience
options.rationale: IM benzathine penicillin G every 28 days is superior to oral penicillin V for secondary prophylaxis due to adherence and bioavailability advantages. Oral prophylaxis is only recommended when IM injections are refused or contraindicated. (Source: RHDAustralia: Secondary Prophylaxis Guideline)

options.text: Continue until at least age 35 or for a minimum of 10 years
options.rationale: For ARF with persistent rheumatic heart disease (even mild), Australian guidelines recommend secondary prophylaxis until age 35 or for a minimum of 10 years (whichever is longer). This child has persistent mild mitral regurgitation, classifying her as having ongoing RHD. Specialist review may extend this further. (Source: RHDAustralia: Secondary Prophylaxis Guideline)

explanation.summary: Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin G is the cornerstone of rheumatic heart disease management. Australian guidelines stratify duration by disease severity: ARF without carditis requires minimum 10 years or until age 21; ARF with carditis but no persistent RHD until age 21 or 10 years minimum; ARF with persistent RHD until age 35 or minimum 10 years. Lifelong prophylaxis is considered for severe RHD or after valve surgery. Adherence to the 28-day injection schedule is critical as even brief gaps increase recurrence risk.
key_points: ARF with persistent RHD: benzathine penicillin G until age 35 or minimum 10 years
IM benzathine penicillin G every 28 days (not every 4 weeks, which drifts beyond 28 days)
Oral penicillin V is inferior and only used when injections are refused or contraindicated
Australia has the highest rates of ARF and RHD among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples globally
explanation.pearls: The 28-day injection interval is critical: extending to every 4 weeks (which accumulates a 1-2 day drift per month) has been associated with ARF recurrence in Australian registries - adherence support and recall systems are essential.

sources.label: Heart Foundation of Australia: ARF and RHD Guideline

subtopic_detail: Nephrotic syndrome: steroid-sparing agent in frequent relapse
stem: A 6-year-old boy with steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome diagnosed at age 4 presents with his fifth relapse in 12 months. Each relapse responds to prednisolone but he relapses within 2 weeks of dose reduction. He has developed cushingoid facies, striae, and his growth velocity has fallen to 3 cm/year. He has no haematuria or hypertension.

data_table.Investigations: Urine protein:creatinine ratio 850 mg/mmol (nephrotic range), albumin 18 g/L, eGFR normal, C3 and C4 normal
data_table.Examination: Cushingoid facies, abdominal striae, periorbital oedema, weight 28 kg (90th centile), height 108 cm (10th centile)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate change to his management?

options.text: Add mycophenolate mofetil as a steroid-sparing maintenance agent
options.rationale: Frequent relapsing nephrotic syndrome (>4 relapses/year) or steroid dependence with significant steroid toxicity warrants a steroid-sparing agent. Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) is a preferred first-line steroid-sparing agent in Australian paediatric nephrology practice due to a favourable side-effect profile compared to cyclophosphamide. (Source: KHA-CARI: Childhood Nephrotic Syndrome)

options.text: Increase prednisolone dose and extend taper over 6 months
options.rationale: Escalating steroid exposure in a child already showing significant steroid toxicity (Cushing features, growth failure) would worsen these complications. A steroid-sparing agent is needed rather than more prednisolone. (Source: KHA-CARI: Childhood Nephrotic Syndrome)

options.text: Commence an ACE inhibitor as the sole antiproteinuric strategy
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce proteinuria but do not prevent relapses in steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome. They are adjunctive in steroid-resistant disease but do not replace immunosuppression in frequent relapsers. (Source: KDIGO: Glomerular Diseases)

options.text: Refer for renal biopsy to reclassify the histological subtype
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is indicated in steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, not steroid-sensitive frequent relapsing disease. This child responds to steroids with each relapse, and the management decision is about steroid-sparing, not reclassification. (Source: KHA-CARI: Childhood Nephrotic Syndrome)

options.text: Give IV cyclophosphamide pulse therapy for definitive remission
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is effective but carries gonadotoxicity risk (cumulative dose-dependent) and is reserved for cases failing first-line steroid-sparing agents. MMF or calcineurin inhibitors are preferred initially due to a better safety profile in children. (Source: KHA-CARI: Childhood Nephrotic Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Frequent relapsing or steroid-dependent nephrotic syndrome affects approximately 50% of children with steroid-sensitive disease. When steroid toxicity develops, steroid-sparing immunosuppressive agents are indicated. The Australian KHA-CARI guidelines recommend mycophenolate mofetil or calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporin, tacrolimus) as first-line steroid-sparing agents. Cyclophosphamide is effective but reserved for refractory cases due to gonadotoxicity. Rituximab is emerging as a second-line option for difficult cases.
key_points: Frequent relapse: 4 or more relapses in 12 months or 2 or more in 6 months
Steroid-sparing agents: MMF or calcineurin inhibitors are first-line
Cyclophosphamide: cumulative dose limit of 168 mg/kg due to gonadotoxicity risk
Monitor growth, bone density, and metabolic complications of prolonged steroid use
explanation.pearls: Mycophenolate mofetil takes 4-6 weeks to reach therapeutic effect in nephrotic syndrome; overlapping prednisolone cover during this period prevents early relapse before MMF efficacy is established.

subtopic_detail: Paediatric migraine: prophylaxis choice
stem: A 10-year-old girl presents with recurrent severe headaches occurring 3-4 times per month for 6 months. Each headache is unilateral, throbbing, associated with nausea and photophobia, lasts 4-8 hours, and prevents school attendance. She has trialled regular paracetamol and ibuprofen at onset with partial relief. She has a family history of migraine in her mother. She has no aura symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination including fundoscopy, visual acuity, and visual fields, BMI 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated given typical presentation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence topiramate 1 mg/kg/day as migraine prophylaxis
options.rationale: Topiramate has evidence for migraine prophylaxis but carries cognitive side effects (word-finding difficulty, reduced processing speed) that may impact school performance. Propranolol or pizotifen are preferred first-line prophylactic agents in Australian paediatric practice. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Commence propranolol 0.5 mg/kg/day with slow dose titration
options.rationale: Propranolol is a first-line migraine prophylactic agent in children per Australian guidelines. It is well-tolerated with a favourable side-effect profile in this age group. The dose is started low and titrated. A 3-month trial is recommended to assess efficacy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: MRI brain to exclude secondary cause before starting therapy
options.rationale: This child has a typical migraine presentation (unilateral, throbbing, nausea, photophobia, family history, normal examination). Neuroimaging is not required when the clinical picture is consistent with primary headache and the neurological examination is normal. (Source: RCH CPG: Headache)

options.text: Commence amitriptyline 10 mg nightly as preventive treatment
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is used for migraine prophylaxis but is generally second-line in children due to anticholinergic side effects and the need for ECG monitoring. It is more commonly used in adolescents with comorbid tension-type headache or mood symptoms. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Prescribe sumatriptan nasal spray for acute migraine attacks
options.rationale: Sumatriptan is effective for acute migraine but this child needs prophylaxis given the frequency (3-4/month) and functional impact. Triptans address acute attacks but do not reduce attack frequency. Prophylaxis should be the priority here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Migraine prophylaxis is indicated in children with frequent migraines (>2-4 per month), significant disability, or inadequate response to acute treatment. Australian guidelines recommend propranolol or pizotifen as first-line prophylactic agents. The prophylactic trial should last at least 3 months before efficacy is assessed. Lifestyle measures including sleep hygiene, regular meals, hydration, and identification of triggers are important adjuncts. Neuroimaging is reserved for atypical features or abnormal examination findings.
key_points: Prophylaxis indicated when migraines occur >2-4 per month with functional impairment
First-line prophylaxis in children: propranolol or pizotifen (Australian guidelines)
Trial duration: minimum 3 months before assessing efficacy
Neuroimaging not required for typical migraine with normal neurological examination
explanation.pearls: Propranolol for paediatric migraine prophylaxis should be avoided in children with asthma; pizotifen (a serotonin antagonist with weight gain as the main side effect) is the preferred alternative in asthmatic children.

subtopic_detail: Guillain-Barre syndrome: IVIG timing and monitoring
stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with progressive lower limb weakness over 4 days following a gastroenteritis illness 2 weeks ago. He initially complained of leg pain, then difficulty walking, and today cannot stand independently. He has no bowel or bladder symptoms. His upper limbs have mild weakness proximally.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral lower limb weakness MRC grade 3/5 proximally and 2/5 distally, upper limbs 4/5 proximally, absent knee and ankle reflexes, flexor plantar responses, no sensory level, normal cranial nerves
data_table.Investigations: CSF: protein 1.2 g/L (ref <0.4), WCC <5 cells, glucose 3.8 mmol/L, nerve conduction studies pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for 5 days
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have not shown benefit in GBS and are not recommended. Despite their role in other inflammatory neurological conditions, steroids do not improve outcomes in GBS and may worsen them. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Begin plasma exchange with 5 sessions over 7 to 10 days
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is an effective treatment for GBS but is technically challenging in children and requires central venous access and specialised equipment. IVIG is preferred in paediatric GBS as first-line due to easier administration and equivalent efficacy. (Source: RCH CPG: Guillain-Barre Syndrome)

options.text: Commence IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days with FVC monitoring
options.rationale: IVIG at 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (total 2 g/kg) is first-line treatment for paediatric GBS when the child cannot walk independently. Forced vital capacity (FVC) monitoring is essential as respiratory failure occurs in up to 20% of children with GBS. FVC <20 mL/kg indicates need for intubation. (Source: RCH CPG: Guillain-Barre Syndrome)

options.text: Observe in hospital with physiotherapy and reassess in 48 hours
options.rationale: This child cannot walk independently, which is the threshold for IVIG treatment in GBS. Observation alone risks progression to respiratory failure. Active immunomodulatory treatment should be commenced promptly alongside monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG: Guillain-Barre Syndrome)

options.text: Commence IVIG 1 g/kg as a single dose with repeat if needed
options.rationale: The standard IVIG regimen for GBS is 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (total 2 g/kg), not a single large dose. The 5-day regimen has the best evidence for efficacy and is better tolerated with fewer infusion-related adverse effects. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Guillain-Barre syndrome in children presents with ascending weakness following an antecedent infection (commonly Campylobacter jejuni or respiratory viruses). IVIG is first-line treatment in children who cannot walk independently. Respiratory monitoring with serial FVC measurements is critical as respiratory failure can develop rapidly. CSF shows albuminocytological dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count). Nerve conduction studies confirm the diagnosis and subtype (AIDP vs AMAN).
key_points: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days is first-line for paediatric GBS when unable to walk
FVC monitoring every 4-6 hours: intubation threshold is FVC <20 mL/kg
CSF: albuminocytological dissociation (protein >0.4 g/L, cells <10)
Autonomic instability (BP fluctuations, arrhythmias) requires cardiac monitoring
explanation.pearls: In paediatric GBS, the FVC decline can be rapid and unpredictable; a falling trend of FVC by >30% from baseline over 24 hours warrants ICU transfer even if the absolute value has not yet reached the intubation threshold of 20 mL/kg.

subtopic_detail: Infantile spasms: vigabatrin versus ACTH selection
stem: A 5-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his mother noticed clusters of brief jerking movements where he flexes his trunk and extends his arms, occurring in clusters of 10-15 on waking. The episodes started 5 days ago and are increasing in frequency. He was born at term with no perinatal complications. His development had been normal until 2 weeks ago when his mother noticed he stopped reaching for objects.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 32, T 36.8 C, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Decreased visual tracking, intermittent flexor spasms observed in clusters, no skin stigmata of tuberous sclerosis
data_table.Investigations: EEG: hypsarrhythmia (high-amplitude chaotic slow waves with multifocal spikes), MRI brain: normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Commence sodium valproate at 20 mg/kg/day in divided doses
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is not first-line for infantile spasms. It has inferior spasm cessation rates compared to hormonal therapy (ACTH or prednisolone) and vigabatrin. Early effective treatment is critical for neurodevelopmental outcomes. (Source: Epilepsy Foundation: Infantile Spasms Guidelines)

options.text: Commence vigabatrin 50 mg/kg/day with ophthalmology review
options.rationale: Vigabatrin is first-line specifically for infantile spasms associated with tuberous sclerosis complex. This child has no clinical or MRI features of TSC. For non-TSC infantile spasms, hormonal therapy (ACTH or high-dose prednisolone) is first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile Spasms)

options.text: Commence levetiracetam 20 mg/kg/day as broad-spectrum agent
options.rationale: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum anticonvulsant but has poor evidence for infantile spasms specifically. The hypsarrhythmia pattern and flexor spasms require targeted therapy (hormonal or vigabatrin) rather than a general anticonvulsant approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile Spasms)

options.text: Commence high-dose prednisolone 15 mg/kg/day for 14 days
options.rationale: For infantile spasms without tuberous sclerosis, hormonal therapy is first-line. Australian practice uses high-dose oral prednisolone (or ACTH) as first-line. The ICISS trial demonstrated high-dose prednisolone has equivalent spasm cessation rates to ACTH and is preferred due to oral administration. Treatment is urgent to limit hypsarrhythmia-related developmental regression. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile Spasms)

options.text: Arrange urgent epilepsy surgery consultation for resection
options.rationale: Epilepsy surgery is considered for infantile spasms with a focal structural lesion on MRI that is potentially resectable. This child has a normal MRI, making surgery inappropriate. Medical therapy is indicated first. (Source: RCH CPG: Infantile Spasms)

explanation.summary: Infantile spasms (West syndrome) is an epileptic encephalopathy characterised by the triad of flexor or extensor spasms, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, and developmental regression. Treatment is time-critical as delayed therapy worsens neurodevelopmental outcomes. First-line treatment depends on aetiology: vigabatrin for tuberous sclerosis-related spasms, and hormonal therapy (high-dose prednisolone or ACTH) for all other causes. The treatment lag from symptom onset to initiation of effective therapy is the strongest modifiable predictor of developmental outcome.
key_points: Hypsarrhythmia + flexor/extensor spasms + developmental regression = West syndrome
Vigabatrin first-line ONLY for tuberous sclerosis-related infantile spasms
Hormonal therapy (high-dose prednisolone or ACTH) is first-line for non-TSC spasms
Treatment lag of >4 weeks from onset significantly worsens neurodevelopmental outcome
explanation.pearls: The ICISS trial showed that adding vigabatrin to hormonal therapy (combination therapy) achieved higher spasm cessation rates (72%) than hormonal therapy alone (57%) at 2 weeks, supporting combination as an emerging standard of care.

subtopic_detail: Sickle cell disease: acute chest syndrome management
stem: A 10-year-old boy with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with fever, pleuritic chest pain, and progressive dyspnoea over 24 hours. He was admitted 2 days ago with a vaso-occlusive crisis affecting his long bones. His baseline haemoglobin is 80 g/L. He is receiving IV morphine and maintenance fluids.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 125, RR 32, SpO2 88% on 6 L/min nasal prongs, T 39.1 C, BP 95/55
data_table.Investigations: Hb 62 g/L (dropped from 80), WCC 18.2 x10^9/L, reticulocytes 2%, CXR: new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, ABG: pH 7.38, pO2 58, pCO2 34
data_table.Examination: Bilateral basal crackles, reduced air entry at both bases, tender chest wall, tachypnoeic and using accessory muscles

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Escalate antibiotics to IV piperacillin-tazobactam and review
options.rationale: Broad-spectrum antibiotics are important but do not address the rapidly falling Hb and hypoxia. The primary intervention needed is to reduce HbS percentage and improve oxygen delivery. Antibiotics should be given alongside the definitive treatment. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula and monitor oxygen saturations
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen is important but does not address the underlying pathology of HbS polymerisation and progressive sickling. With dropping Hb, bilateral infiltrates, and hypoxia on 6 L, the child needs transfusion to reduce HbS concentration. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone to reduce pulmonary inflammation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids in acute chest syndrome are controversial. While some studies show reduced length of stay, rebound vaso-occlusive crises occur frequently after cessation. They are not recommended as primary therapy and do not substitute for transfusion. (Source: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell)
source_refs: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell

options.text: Perform an urgent whole blood exchange via femoral line access
options.rationale: Automated red cell exchange (erythrocytapheresis) is indicated for severe or rapidly deteriorating ACS, but simple top-up transfusion should be attempted first when Hb is significantly below baseline. Exchange transfusion is reserved for Hb >90 g/L where simple transfusion would cause hyperviscosity. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

options.text: Urgent simple red cell transfusion to raise Hb and reduce HbS
options.rationale: With Hb dropped to 62 g/L (below baseline of 80), worsening hypoxia, and bilateral infiltrates, urgent simple transfusion is indicated. The target is Hb 90-100 g/L (not >100 to avoid hyperviscosity). Simple transfusion is appropriate as the Hb is well below baseline. Phenotyped, sickle-negative, crossmatched blood should be used. (Source: HSANZ: Sickle Cell Disease Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute chest syndrome is the leading cause of death in sickle cell disease. It is defined as a new pulmonary infiltrate on CXR with at least one of: fever, respiratory symptoms, or hypoxia. Management includes supplemental oxygen, antibiotics (covering atypical organisms), analgesia (avoiding over-sedation), incentive spirometry, and transfusion. Simple transfusion is indicated when Hb is below baseline; exchange transfusion is reserved for severe ACS with Hb near baseline or rapidly deteriorating gas exchange despite simple transfusion.
key_points: ACS diagnosis: new CXR infiltrate + fever or respiratory symptoms or hypoxia in sickle cell disease
Simple transfusion when Hb is below baseline; target Hb 90-100 g/L (never >100 g/L to avoid hyperviscosity)
Exchange transfusion for severe/deteriorating ACS or when Hb is near baseline (>90 g/L)
Antibiotics must cover atypical organisms (macrolide + beta-lactam)
explanation.pearls: In ACS, a low reticulocyte count (<2%) in the context of a falling Hb suggests aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19 co-infection) superimposed on ACS, which mandates urgent transfusion as the bone marrow is unable to compensate for the acute haemolysis.

sources.label: British Society for Haematology: Sickle Cell

subtopic_detail: Thalassaemia trait versus iron deficiency discrimination
stem: A 3-year-old boy of Mediterranean heritage is found to have a microcytic anaemia on routine blood tests. His parents are concerned as he has been a fussy eater with low meat intake. He has no history of blood loss, pica, or chronic illness. His growth and development are normal.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L, MCV 62 fL (ref 70-86), MCH 22 pg (ref 24-30), RCC 5.8 x10^12/L (ref 4.0-5.5), RDW 13.5% (ref 11.5-14.5), ferritin 45 mcg/L (ref 15-200), iron 12 micromol/L (ref 9-21), transferrin saturation 22%

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Beta-thalassaemia trait with normal iron stores confirmed
